drainage and low-lying areas.

Danville, Vermilion Regional Airport, Bismarck, Henning and Alvin.

properties.headline: Special Weather Statement issued June 6 at 11:56PM EDT by NWS Newport/Morehead City NC
properties.description:
Abnormally dry fuel conditions further exacerbated by high
temperatures reaching well into the 90s inland, low relative
humidity (lowest away from the immediate coast), and strengthening
southwest winds gusting to 15-20mph in the afternoon will lead to
increased fire danger Sunday.

Fire fuels could easily ignite and spread rapidly in these
conditions. Residents and visitors to the area are urged to
properly dispose of cigarette butts, matches, and any other
flammable items in appropriate containers. Exercise caution if
operating any equipment that could create sparks, and keep
vehicles off dry leaves and grass.

Please refer to your local burn permitting authorities on whether
you can burn. If you do, you are advised to use extreme caution.

...FIRE WEATHER WATCH REMAINS IN EFFECT FROM MONDAY MORNING
THROUGH TUESDAY EVENING FOR GUSTY WINDS, LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY
AND DRY FUELS FOR FIRE WEATHER ZONES 200, 202, 207, 290, 292,
486, 487, 490, AND 491...

...RED FLAG WARNING WILL EXPIRE AT 9 PM MDT THIS EVENING FOR
GUSTY WINDS, LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY AND DRY FUELS FOR FIRE WEATHER
ZONES 200, 202, 207, 290, 292, 486, 487, 490, AND 491...

* AFFECTED AREA...In Colorado, Fire Weather Zone 200 Little
Snake Forecast Area, Fire Weather Zone 202 White River
Forecast Area, Fire Weather Zone 207 Southwest Colorado Lower
Forecast Area, Fire Weather Zone 290 Paradox Valley Forecast
Area and Fire Weather Zone 292 North Fork Forecast Area. In
Utah, Fire Weather Zone 486 Eastern Uinta Basin, Fire Weather
Zone 487 Book Cliffs, Fire Weather Zone 490 Colorado River
Basin and Fire Weather Zone 491 Southeast Utah.

* TIMING...For the Red Flag Warning, from 10 AM to 10 PM MDT
Sunday. For the Fire Weather Watch, from Monday morning
through Tuesday evening.

* WINDS...Southwest 20 to 30 mph with gusts up to 50 mph.

* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...5 to 10 percent.

* IMPACTS...Fires will catch and spread quickly. Exercise
extreme caution with any outdoor burning.

properties.areaDesc: Wayne; Sampson
properties.headline: Special Weather Statement issued June 6 at 8:17PM EDT by NWS Raleigh NC
properties.description:
The combination of low relative humidity around 25 to 30 percent,
breezy winds up to 10 to 20 mph, and extremely dry fuels will
lead to an increased fire danger on Sunday for portions of
eastern North Carolina.

Dry grasses, dead leaves, and other tree litter could easily
ignite and spread fire quickly. People in this area are urged to
properly dispose of cigarette butts, matches, and any other
flammable items in appropriate containers. Exercise caution if
operating any equipment that could create sparks, and keep
vehicles off of dry leaves and grass.

properties.headline: Beach Hazards Statement issued June 6 at 8:14PM EDT until June 7 at 8:00PM EDT by NWS Wilmington NC
properties.description:

Moderate Risk of rip currents expected.

* WHERE...Coastal Pender and Coastal New Hanover Counties.

* WHEN...From 6 AM EDT Sunday through Sunday evening.

* IMPACTS...Longshore currents can sweep swimmers and surfers into
rip currents, piers, jetties, and other hazardous areas. It may
sweep swimmers off their feet, making it difficult to return to
shore.

properties.areaDesc: Hertford; Gates; Pasquotank; Camden; Western Currituck; Bertie; Chowan; Perquimans; Eastern Currituck
properties.headline: Special Weather Statement issued June 6 at 8:11PM EDT by NWS Wakefield VA
properties.description:
Dry, hot, and breezy conditions are expected Sunday. The ongoing
drought has led to abnormally dry dead surface fuels across the
region. Min RH values of 25 to 35%, wind gusts to around 20 mph,
and afternoon high temperatures in the low to mid 90s ahead of a
cold front will lead to increased fire danger from mid morning
through early evening Sunday.

Residents are urged to exercise caution handling any potential
ignition source...including machinery... cigarettes...and matches.
Be sure to properly discard all smoking materials. Any dry grasses
and tree litter that ignite will have the potential to spread
quickly.

properties.areaDesc: Central Brooks Range
properties.headline: Winter Weather Advisory issued June 6 at 12:58PM AKDT until June 8 at 10:00PM AKDT by NWS Fairbanks AK
properties.description:
* WHAT...Snow expected. Total snow accumulations between 5 and 10
inches. Winds gusting as high as 30 mph at Atigun Pass.

* WHERE...Central Brooks Range.

* WHEN...From 10 PM this evening to 10 PM AKDT Monday.

* IMPACTS...Travel could be difficult. Gusty winds could lead to
areas of blowing snow which could significantly reduce visibility.

* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...An unseasonably strong arctic trough will
pass over the North Slope and Brooks Range Sunday into Monday.
Widespread light snow is expected across the Arctic Coast and
Arctic Plains, with highest totals in the Brooks Range and
Southern Arctic Plains. Winds will be strongest over Atigun Pass,
where gusts up to 30 mph are expected. Heaviest snow accumulations
are expected along the Dalton Highway from Chandalar Shelf over
Atigun Pass to Toolik Lake and Ice Cut Hill. Locally higher totals
are expected above pass level, with lighter totals down at
Anaktuvuk Pass and south of Chandalar Shelf.

properties.headline: Air Quality Alert issued June 6 at 12:40PM CDT by NWS La Crosse WI
properties.description:
* WHAT...The Minnesota Pollution Control Agency has issued an Air Quality
Alert for ozone pollution. The Air Quality Index (AQI) is expected
to reach the Orange or Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups category.

* WHERE...Southern, central, and western Minnesota. This includes
Dodge, Fillmore, Houston, Mower, Olmsted, Wabasha, and Winona
counties in southeast Minnesota.

* WHEN...Until 11 PM CDT Sunday.

* IMPACTS...Sensitive groups, such as people with lung disease
(including asthma), heart disease, children and older adults, and
people who are active outdoors, may experience health effects.

* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Ground-level ozone is expected to be high Saturday
and Sunday afternoon across much of Minnesota. Mostly sunny skies,
warm temperatures, and low humidity will provide a favorable
environment for two types of pollutants (volatile organic compounds
and nitrogen oxides) to react with sunlight in the air to produce
ground-level ozone. The ozone levels will decrease after sunset
Saturday and be lower Sunday morning before increasing again Sunday
afternoon. Consider moving outdoor activities outside of the
afternoon hours.
